text: A woman sued a fast-food chain after spilling hot coffee on herself.
verdict: Real — she won. The coffee was dangerously hot. (Liebeck v. McDonald's, 1994)

text: A man sued a beer company claiming their adverts falsely promised him a fun social life.
verdict: Real — he lost. The court was not amused.

text: A burglar sued homeowners after getting stuck in their garage and living on dog food for days.
verdict: Real — and he actually won a settlement.

text: Someone sued themselves and asked the state to pay the damages.
verdict: Real — it happened.

text: A man tried to divorce his wife by delivering the papers via carrier pigeon.
verdict: Fake — carrier pigeons are not recognised process servers.

text: A company was sued because its 'magic diet tea' did not, in fact, teleport fat away.
verdict: Fake — no quantum weight-loss products have reached court.

text: A man sued God for the weather and asked for an injunction against storms.
verdict: Real — the case was dismissed for lack of service.

text: A prisoner sued himself for violating his own civil rights and demanded $5 million.
verdict: Real — he asked the state to pay since he was a ward of the state.

text: A woman sued a zoo after a peacock screamed at her and ruined her marriage proposal.
verdict: Fake — peacocks are dramatic, but not legally liable.

text: A tenant sued their landlord because the house was haunted and no ghosts were disclosed.
verdict: Fake — no material-ghost disclosure requirement yet.

text: A judge once ruled a burrito is not a sandwich.
verdict: Real — the famous 'what is a sandwich' case set food-law precedent.

text: A company trademarked the sound of a chainsaw starting.
verdict: Fake — though some sounds are trademarked, this one is not.

text: A man sued a dry cleaner for $67 million over a missing pair of trousers.
verdict: Real — he lost and the case became legendary.

text: A city council banned ice cream trucks after one gave a judge brain freeze.
verdict: Fake — judges have ruled through worse.

text: A woman sued Google Maps after she walked onto a motorway following its directions.
verdict: Real — she sued for being led into traffic.

text: A cat was called as a witness in a murder trial and had to 'swear' on a Bible.
verdict: Fake — cats make unreliable witnesses.

text: A student sued their university for not warning them that law school was hard.
verdict: Fake — but the feeling is real.

text: A court once had to decide whether a Jaffa Cake was a cake or a biscuit for tax purposes.
verdict: Real — it was ruled a cake, saving it from higher biscuit tax.

text: A lawyer objected to his own question in the middle of asking it.
verdict: Fake — but every litigator has wanted to.

text: A man sued his neighbour because their dog mocked him by copying his walk.
verdict: Fake — no legal remedy for canine impressions.
